EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of Thursday, September 20th, a warm front lifted northward across far southern Minnesota. By the late afternoon, the warm front lied from just south of the Minnesota River, northeast to around Wabasha along the Mississippi River. ||Several thunderstorms developed in southwest Minnesota, and northwest Iowa. These storms moved into far southern Minnesota and quickly intensified and raced off to the east-northeast along, and north of the warm front. ||A storm survey indicated that a tornado formed just east of the Mississippi River near Red Wing, and tracked into Pierce county. Another tornado damaged the town of Ellsworth. There was also sporadic wind damage around the city of Spring Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey concluded that an EF0 tornado tracked from the Village of Ellsworth, northeast for 1.67 miles before dissipating. Damage was confined to large trees blown down or uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms fired along a stalled out frontal boundary located to the south of the CWA in a very moist and unstable airmass. A few of the storms skirted the southern reaches of Price County and contained damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down south of Kennan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second straight day, severe storms moved across western Wisconsin on August 28th. These storms produced damaging winds, large hail and another round of heavy rain. This additional heavy rain aggravated ongoing flooding and caused some new flooding to occur. Water from the West Fork of the Kickapoo River covered roads near Avalanche (Vernon County). Trees were blown down in Viroqua (Vernon County), Lyndon Station (Juneau County) and Plainville (Adams County). Thousands of trees were blown down across southern Adams County in a 3 to 4 mile wide area that started just south of the State Highways 13 and 82 intersection and continued east across Springville and Jackson Townships to the Marquette County line east of Jordan Lake. Houses were damaged by trees that landed on them and boats were blown were dislodged from docks and floated out into Jordan Lake. The only hail that was reported was quarter sized south of Brooks (Adams County). Because of the flooding along the Kickapoo and Pine Rivers, a state of emergency was declared in Viola (Vernon and Richland Counties) and all of Richland County. The State of Wisconsin Governor declared a state of emergency for the entire state. A federal disaster declaration was made for La Crosse, Monroe, Juneau, Adams, Vernon, Crawford and Richland Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of trees were blown down in Viroqua.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few storms developed ahead of a cold front in a warm and moist airmass. One of the storms took on supercellular characteristics over northern Price County. The storm split with the left mover quickly dissipating while the right mover produced large hail and damaging winds before moving out of the CWA.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms moved across western Wisconsin during the evening of August 27th. A brief tornado touched down and had an intermittent damage path between Tomah and Wyeville (Monroe County). The damage was limited to trees and power poles. These storms blew down trees and caused roof damage in Sparta (Monroe County), rolled a camper and damaged several signs near Tomah (Monroe County) and downed trees around Hixton (Jackson County) and in the Town of Cleveland (Jackson County). The  storms then became oriented in an east to west line and dumped copious amounts of rain from southern La Crosse County and northern Vernon County east across the southern sections of Monroe, Juneau and Adams Counties. Numerous roads were closed because of flooding or mudslides, homes had to be evacuated with several water rescues were performed. Towns that were hit hard by the flooding included Coon Valley, Ontario (Vernon County), Elroy and Wonewoc (Juneau County). The eastbound Amtrak Empire Builder had to be halted near Tomah because of a washout on the rail line near Mauston (Juneau County). In Monroe and Vernon Counties, four earthen flood control dams failed and seven others sustained damage. Rainfall totals of 6 to 10 inches were common in this heavy rain band with the highest reported totals being 9.14 inches in Union Center (Juneau County) and 9.98 inches near Westby (Vernon County). The runoff later produced historic flooding on the Kickapoo River with several record flood crests established. Damages in Juneau County exceeded $22 million of which $6.5 million was from Elroy. The State of Wisconsin Governor declared a state of emergency for the entire state.  A federal disaster declaration was made for La Crosse, Monroe, Juneau, Adams, Vernon, Crawford and Richland Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down in Sparta and several of these landed on houses. Part of a roof was blown off and several power lines were also blown down.

The afternoon of Sunday, August 26th, a line of storms that developed across southeast Minnesota, moved rapidly northeast across west central Wisconsin. One of these storms produced damaging winds near Chippewa Falls, Wisconsin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms became more organized as they moved just south of the Twin Cities Metro area. Clusters of storms developed into a bow echo as it raced northeast into west central Wisconsin. The Red Wing, Minnesota airport (located in Wisconsin) measured a wind gust of 82 mph as this bow segment moved into Pierce County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees were snapped off in Hager City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms moved across western Wisconsin during the evening of August 27th. A brief tornado touched down and had an intermittent damage path between Tomah and Wyeville (Monroe County). The damage was limited to trees and power poles. These storms blew down trees and caused roof damage in Sparta (Monroe County), rolled a camper and damaged several signs near Tomah (Monroe County) and downed trees around Hixton (Jackson County) and in the Town of Cleveland (Jackson County). The  storms then became oriented in an east to west line and dumped copious amounts of rain from southern La Crosse County and northern Vernon County east across the southern sections of Monroe, Juneau and Adams Counties. Numerous roads were closed because of flooding or mudslides, homes had to be evacuated with several water rescues were performed. Towns that were hit hard by the flooding included Coon Valley, Ontario (Vernon County), Elroy and Wonewoc (Juneau County). The eastbound Amtrak Empire Builder had to be halted near Tomah because of a washout on the rail line near Mauston (Juneau County). In Monroe and Vernon Counties, four earthen flood control dams failed and seven others sustained damage. Rainfall totals of 6 to 10 inches were common in this heavy rain band with the highest reported totals being 9.14 inches in Union Center (Juneau County) and 9.98 inches near Westby (Vernon County). The runoff later produced historic flooding on the Kickapoo River with several record flood crests established. Damages in Juneau County exceeded $22 million of which $6.5 million was from Elroy. The State of Wisconsin Governor declared a state of emergency for the entire state.  A federal disaster declaration was made for La Crosse, Monroe, Juneau, Adams, Vernon, Crawford and Richland Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rains produced street flooding in various locations around the city of La Crosse.  Travel in the city was severely impacted, water flowed into people's yards and lawns, and some cars became stranded in deeper intersections.

The BNSF rail line was closed because of washouts between La Crosse and the Vernon County line. Sections of Highways 14/61 in the southern end of the county were damaged and had mudslides over them.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain caused flash flooding to occur across the southern sections of Monroe County. Near Melvina, a resident had to be rescued from a home surrounded by flood waters. Flooding was extreme along the Little La Crosse River along and just east of Highway 27 between Cashton and Sparta where numerous homes were flooded, rescues were needed, and roads were damaged.  Flooding along Moore Creek in Norwalk damaged some ballfields and the village office. In Kendall, a park along the Baraboo River was destroyed and a newly installed road to the village's wastewater treatment plant was washed out. Most of the flood damaged homes and business were along Glen Street. Across the county, 75 people had to be evacuated. At the Skogdalen cemetery, 90% of the gravestones markers were toppled, debris was up to 5 feet deep in places and ripped apart the road in front of the church. Numerous roads were closed, with most of these in the far southern part of the county in Portland, Jefferson, Sheldon, Wellington and Glendale Townships. Some of the impacted roads included: County Highway N, County Highway Z south of Wilton, County Highway P near Kendall, County Highway F south of Norwalk, County Highway D near Cashton, County Highway CM near Tomah, State Highway 33 east of Cashton, County Highway W north of Kendall, County Highway ET east of Tomah and County Highway F east of Melvina. The heavy rain and flooding caused three earthen flood control dams to fail and damaged three others.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain caused extensive flooding to occur in Elroy from the Baraboo River. Between 25 and 30 homes had to be evacuated. Numerous homes had water several inches to a few feet deep in the basements. Nine homes likely will have to be torn down. Damages in Elroy totaled nearly $6.5 million. The flooding along the Baraboo River also hit Wonewoc hard causing officials to evacuate the western half of the town. Dozens of homes had water in the basements with at least 4 of them suffering collapsed walls. Flooding along the Lemonweir River near Mauston caused Interstates 90/94 to be closed in both directions and washed out a rail line that caused the eastbound Amtrak Empire Builder to be stopped near Tomah. Passengers had to remain on the stopped train overnight until repairs to the rail line were completed. Flooding in Mauston damaged some homes with at least one a total loss when a basement wall collapsed. Flood waters from Webster Creek covered portions of State Highway 80 between New Lisbon and Elroy. State Highway 33 was under water in places between Union Center and Wonewoc because of flood waters from the Baraboo River. State Highway 82 was closed between Elroy and Mauston due to flood waters from Cleaver Creek. Portions of State Highway 58 south of Mauston was covered in flood waters from Onemile and Stewart Creeks. U.S. Highway 12 was closed between Mauston and Lyndon Station because of flooding on the Lemonweir River. County highways across the southern part of the county that were closed included: N, G, HH and FF. Numerous local and township roads were also closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms became more organized as they moved just south of the Twin Cities Metro area. Clusters of storms developed into a bow echo as it raced northeast into west central Wisconsin. The Red Wing, Minnesota airport (located in Wisconsin) measured a wind gust of 82 mph as this bow segment moved into Pierce County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down in Spring Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second straight day, severe storms moved across western Wisconsin on August 28th. These storms produced damaging winds, large hail and another round of heavy rain. This additional heavy rain aggravated ongoing flooding and caused some new flooding to occur. Water from the West Fork of the Kickapoo River covered roads near Avalanche (Vernon County). Trees were blown down in Viroqua (Vernon County), Lyndon Station (Juneau County) and Plainville (Adams County). Thousands of trees were blown down across southern Adams County in a 3 to 4 mile wide area that started just south of the State Highways 13 and 82 intersection and continued east across Springville and Jackson Townships to the Marquette County line east of Jordan Lake. Houses were damaged by trees that landed on them and boats were blown were dislodged from docks and floated out into Jordan Lake. The only hail that was reported was quarter sized south of Brooks (Adams County). Because of the flooding along the Kickapoo and Pine Rivers, a state of emergency was declared in Viola (Vernon and Richland Counties) and all of Richland County. The State of Wisconsin Governor declared a state of emergency for the entire state. A federal disaster declaration was made for La Crosse, Monroe, Juneau, Adams, Vernon, Crawford and Richland Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rains caused extensive flooding to occur along the Kickapoo River that resulted in numerous road closings. State Highway 131 was closed in several locations as it runs parallel to the river. Other road closures included: State Highway 179 in Steuben, State Highway 171 in Gays Mills, State Highway 60 near Wauzeka, County Highway B north of Gays Mills, County Highway C in Soldiers Grove, County Highway S south of Bell Center and County Highway W between Bell Center and Steuben. One water rescue had to be performed when a elderly man drove his vehicle into flood waters.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain across the northern portion of Vernon County produced significant flash flooding. Over 200 people were evacuated in Coon Valley because of severe flooding along Coon Creek where U.S. Highway 14 and State Highway 162 were closed. All access into Coon Valley was cut off because of mudslides, washouts and closed roads. Numerous homes were severely damaged with basement walls blown out or had water several feet deep on the first floor. Much of Ontario was covered in flood waters from the Kickapoo River and Brush Creek with almost every business along Garden Street, State Highway 131 and the east end of Main Street sustaining damage. It was estimated that around 75% of the businesses in the entire village sustained some damage. The Ontario police squad car become submerged in flood waters and was destroyed. One boat rental business lost 40 canoes and 48 kayaks. Two campers along the Kickapoo River north of Rockton had to be rescued from flood waters that was neck high. Some residents were evacuated from Viola because of flooding along the Kickapoo River. A total of 350 water rescues were performed across the county. State Highway 131 was closed between Ontario and Rockton because of flood waters from the Kickapoo River where the asphalt was completely removed from the road bed in places or broken in large chunks and then moved up to eight feet from its original location. Numerous roads were closed because of the flooding or from mudslides. Flooding from Timber Coulee Creek washed out over 20 power poles north of Westby. The BNSF rail line was closed because of washouts between Stoddard and the La Crosse County line. The heavy rain and flooding caused two earthen flood control dams to fail and damaged four others.  This prompted the evacuation and rescue of residents downstream from the dams, including the communities of Avalanche and Liberty. Wildcat Mountain State Park was closed because of the heavy rain as well.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain created historic flooding along the Kickapoo River in Readstown. The river established a new crest of record at 23.17 feet, which was over 12 feet above the flood stage. The flooding continued into early September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms moved across western Wisconsin during the evening of August 27th. A brief tornado touched down and had an intermittent damage path between Tomah and Wyeville (Monroe County). The damage was limited to trees and power poles. These storms blew down trees and caused roof damage in Sparta (Monroe County), rolled a camper and damaged several signs near Tomah (Monroe County) and downed trees around Hixton (Jackson County) and in the Town of Cleveland (Jackson County). The  storms then became oriented in an east to west line and dumped copious amounts of rain from southern La Crosse County and northern Vernon County east across the southern sections of Monroe, Juneau and Adams Counties. Numerous roads were closed because of flooding or mudslides, homes had to be evacuated with several water rescues were performed. Towns that were hit hard by the flooding included Coon Valley, Ontario (Vernon County), Elroy and Wonewoc (Juneau County). The eastbound Amtrak Empire Builder had to be halted near Tomah because of a washout on the rail line near Mauston (Juneau County). In Monroe and Vernon Counties, four earthen flood control dams failed and seven others sustained damage. Rainfall totals of 6 to 10 inches were common in this heavy rain band with the highest reported totals being 9.14 inches in Union Center (Juneau County) and 9.98 inches near Westby (Vernon County). The runoff later produced historic flooding on the Kickapoo River with several record flood crests established. Damages in Juneau County exceeded $22 million of which $6.5 million was from Elroy. The State of Wisconsin Governor declared a state of emergency for the entire state.  A federal disaster declaration was made for La Crosse, Monroe, Juneau, Adams, Vernon, Crawford and Richland Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain created historic flooding along the Kickapoo River in La Farge. The river established a new crest of record that was estimated to be 19.42 feet, which was over 7 feet above the flood stage.

Two to three days of hot and humid conditions produced heat index values in the 100's. Numerous cooling centers were opened by local communities throughout southern WI. Some public swimming pools hours were extended due to the heat. The heatwave continued into July 1st over far southeast WI.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front was located across central Minnesota into southwest Wisconsin. Low level flow maintained the influx of moisture and warm air as a shortwave topped a ridge in place to the west. This led to storms throughout much of the day, a few of which were severe with damaging winds and hail. The storms also contained heavy rainfall which primed parts of the area for major flash flooding the following night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail lasted about 10 minutes.

The third and final day of a three day severe weather event across the Northland. A stalled out frontal system finally made a push across the area leading to another round of severe storms and heavy rainfall over areas that had seen dramatic flash flooding the previous night. Widespread rainfall amounts of 1-2 inches plus over northeastern Minnesota and northwestern Wisconsin sent already swollen creeks and rivers out of their banks and over roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of showers and thunderstorms developed along the Mississippi River and then moved slowly southeast across southwest Wisconsin during the late evening of June 8th into the early morning of the 9th. Runoff from these storms produced some flash flooding that caused a mudslide that covered a portion of County Road A near Bagley and debris and water covered roads near Beetown.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain pushed the Platte River out of its banks near Rockville. The river crested over four feet above the flood stage at 13.11 feet.

Two rounds of thunderstorms moved across portions of western Wisconsin during the late afternoon and evening of June 15th. These storms occasionally produced some damaging winds that blew down trees from near Whitehall (Trempealeau County) to Camp Douglas (Juneau County). Half dollar sized hail also fell in Oakdale (Monroe County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down southwest of Whitehall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a cold front moved slowly into western Wisconsin, numerous thunderstorms developed along the front during the late afternoon and evening of June 17th. Some of the late afternoon thunderstorms spawned funnel clouds that were visible near Willard and Loyal (Clark Counties). Some of the storms produced damaging winds that blew down trees across the northern sections of Buffalo County into Clark County. A trained spotter estimated a 70 mph wind gust west of Spencer (Clark County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Funnel clouds were sighted near Willard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: June ended with two days of excessive heat across western Wisconsin. Heat indices topped out between 105 and 115 on both June 29th and 30th, although the high heat indices were more widespread on the 29th. Air temperatures in the upper 80s to middle 90s with dew points in the middle 70s combined to create the excessive heat. The highest calculated heat index was 115 from mesonet stations in Mauston (Juneau County) and De Soto (Crawford County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices of 105 to 115 were common across Buffalo County on June 29th and 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two complexes of thunderstorms moved across southwest Wisconsin during the late evening of June 9th into the early morning hours of the 10th. These storms produced locally heavy rains with the runoff resulting in some flash flooding across Grant County. Several rural roads were covered with water in the vicinity of Stitzer and Ellenboro. U.S. Highway 61 was closed between Fennimore and Lancaster and up to a foot of water covered State Highway 81 near Ellenboro. Three people had to be rescued from vehicles southwest of Ellenboro. The highest reported rainfall total was 6.6 inches in Fennimore.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rains pushed several small streams out of their banks across central Grant County. U.S. Highway 61 had to be closed between Lancaster and Fennimore because of the flooding. State Highway 81 near Ellenboro had up to a foot of water on it. At least one home near Stitzer had water in the basement. Three people were rescued from vehicles on Vesperman Road near Ellenboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After one of the wettest May's on record, above normal rainfall continued in June. Some rivers reached minor flood stage. The Yahara chain of lakes and surrounding marshland reached very high levels. Lake Kegonsa set a new high water record. Mitigation was needed to protect property for homes on the northeast side of Lake Kegonsa and a few homes on Lake Waubesa near Goodland Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of heavy rain in June led to very high water levels on the Yahara chain of lakes and surrounding marshland. Residents on the northeast side of Lake Kegonsa and on Lake Waubesa near Goodland Park were able to mitigate the effects of the high water by employing 8500 sandbags, and removing boats, boat lifts, and piers. Approximately 17 homes and their lake property, mainly on Sunnyside St. and Williams Dr. near Lake Kegonsa and the Door Creek wetland area, were protected by the mitigation efforts. Flooding from the Door Creek wetland area posed a greater risk than a record breaking level of 845.75 inches on Lake Kegonsa. Mitigation was also employed for a few homes on Lake Waubesa near Goodland Park. Only minor flooding occurred to some of the homes that were in the high water areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two to three days of hot and humid conditions produced heat index values in the 100's. Numerous cooling centers were opened by local communities throughout southern WI. Some public swimming pools hours were extended due to the heat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat index values ranging from 100 to 117 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With the ground already saturated from rain that fell in September, flash flooding occurred on the first day of October after a round of thunderstorms moved across southwest Wisconsin. Rainfall amounts of 1.5 to 3 inches were common across Grant County and the runoff from this rain caused water to go over State Highway 81 east of Cassville and near Ellenboro. Water also went over County Highways E and O along with some township roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rains pushed the Grant River out of its banks near Burton. The river crested over six feet above the flood stage on October 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: December started with an early season snow storm across western Wisconsin. A round of snow during the morning of December 1st was followed by a second round during the evening of the 1st into the early morning of the 2nd. These two rounds deposited between 5 and 8 inches of snow across Monroe County. This snow was accompanied by wind gusts of 35 to 40 mph and drifting of the heavy, wet snow. The highest reported snow total was 8 inches near Tomah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that between 5 and 8 inches of snow fell across Monroe County. The highest reported total was 8 inches near Tomah. Wind gusts of 35 to 40 mph created drifting of the heavy, wet snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: December 2018 ended with parts of western Wisconsin receiving heavy snow. Snow fell for much of New Year's Eve day with totals of 6 to 7 inches common across the northern half of Richland County and over much of Adams and Juneau Counties. The highest reported total was 7 inches near Mauston (Juneau County). The precipitation started as a short period of freezing drizzle that lasted long enough to create a glaze of ice on untreated or elevated surfaces.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported around 6 inches of snow across the southern two-thirds of Adams County. The highest reported total was 6.8 inches near White Creek. The precipitation started as a period of light freezing drizzle with a glaze if ice on untreated and elevated surfaces.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure passed to the south of the area and led to a prolonged snow event that lasted parts of 3 days in the days just after Christmas. All of northeast Minnesota and the northwest corner of Wisconsin were affected by this storm. Snowfall amounts were generally in the 8 to 16 inch range, although there were pockets of higher amounts. Much of northwest Wisconsin saw snow, but they also saw rain, which considerably cut back on the snow amounts.

Water is in park land near the river in Fort Atkinson.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sections of the isthmus in Madison, including many streets,  experienced prolonged flooding due to record high water levels on Lake Mendota and Lake Monona. Much of the flooding was near the Yahara River which connects Lake Mendota to Lake Monona. Additional flooding occurred in lakeshore areas of Lake Mendota, Lake Monona, and Lake Waubesa. Thousands of sandbags were deployed to prevent flood damages. Streets, bike paths, parks, boat launches and piers were closed in the isthmus and other areas in the Yahara Chain of Lakes.

Lake Koshkonong merged with Mud Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of strong to severe thunderstorms developed near a surface trough with some tree damage occurring. Training thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall and street flooding in Sheboygan.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding in Sheboygan especially on and near 14th street from Geele Ave. to Indiana Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms from the approach of a strong cold front resulted in straight-line wind damage in the Village of Sullivan. Numerous tree damage occurred and structural damage to a storage building. The training of thunderstorms also resulted in the flash flooding and closure of some streets in Lafayette County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portions of a roof blown off a storage building. Numerous large tree branches down throughout Sullivan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: River flooding was prevalent across southern WI during the month of September after receiving 12 to 20 inches of rain from August 16th to September 5th. Additional rains throughout the remainder of the month prolonged the flooding. Moderate to major flood stage was reached on the Baraboo and Fox Rivers of central WI, and the Rock River in Jefferson and Rock Counties. Flooding continued into early September on sections of the isthmus in Madison due the high lake levels of Mendota and Monona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rock River at Fort Atkinson reached moderate flood stage, cresting at 17.31 feet the evening of September 11th. Numerous roads are flooded and closed in the Fort Atkinson area, including Sinnissippi Dr. in Rock River Park, Bark River Rd. from Kutz Road to Rock River Rd., North Shore Rd., and Ralph Rd.  Floodwaters surround a few homes and businesses close to the river at the end of Veterans Lane near the intersection of Highway 106 and Blackhawk Island Rd. Floodwaters are in the backyards of some homes on Rogers St. The river walk in downtown Fort Atkinson is flooded and closed.

Center Ave. Riverview Drive near the wastewater plant is flooded and closed. Floodwaters affect portions of Highway K between Jefferson and Fort Atkinson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: River flooding was prevalent across southern WI during the month of September after receiving 12 to 20 inches of rain from August 16th to September 5th. Additional rains throughout the remainder of the month prolonged the flooding. Moderate to major flood stage was reached on the Baraboo and Fox Rivers of central WI, and the Rock River in Jefferson and Rock Counties. Flooding continued into early September on sections of the isthmus in Madison due the high lake levels of Mendota and Monona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Montello River flooded portions of Montello as it flowed out of Lake Montello. Major flooding occurred and the Main St. bridge was closed due to the high water of the river impacting the bridge. Approximately, 150 homes and businesses were flooded. Much of the flooding occurred on or near Montello St., Island Dr., Park St., Main St., and portions of Cass St. The first week of school was delayed due to the transportation limitations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms from the approach of a strong cold front resulted in straight-line wind damage in the Village of Sullivan. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few roads closed in Belmont and Darlington due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A west to east band of heavy rainfall near a stationary front from Grant County east to Rock County caused minor flooding of the Pecatonica and Sugar Rivers. Over 2 inches of rain fell over the aforementioned areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pecatonica River at Martintown reached minor flood stage, cresting at 14.57 feet the afternoon of September 25th. Floodwaters affected Martintown Road and West River Road in the Martintown area. Upstream in Browntown, floodwaters affected highway MM and West Indies Road. Farm fields were also flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A west to east band of heavy rainfall near a stationary front from Grant County east to Rock County caused minor flooding of the Pecatonica and Sugar Rivers. Over 2 inches of rain fell over the aforementioned areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sugar River reached minor flood stage, cresting at 6.26 feet the afternoon of September 22nd. Some roads are flooded in the Avon Township. There is flooding in Sugar River Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A teenager drowns in Lake Michigan during a period of dangerous swimming conditions due to high winds and waves.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A male teenager jumped into Lake Michigan at the north pier in Kenosha Wisconsin during a period when strong winds and high waves were creating dangerous swimming conditions on Lake Michigan. The teenager disappeared under the waves due to structural and rip currents under the water. A second person also jumped into the water, but was able to climb out. The teenager's body was found 6 days later several miles north of the pier along the Lake Michigan shore near Carthage College.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: River flooding was prevalent across southern WI during the month of September after receiving 12 to 20 inches of rain from August 16th to September 5th. Additional rains throughout the remainder of the month prolonged the flooding. Moderate to major flood stage was reached on the Baraboo and Fox Rivers of central WI, and the Rock River in Jefferson and Rock Counties. Flooding continued into early September on sections of the isthmus in Madison due the high lake levels of Mendota and Monona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Milwaukee River crested at minor flood stage at 12.2 feet during the early afternoon of August 29th, but continued flooding into the evening of September 3rd. Floodwaters spread into the backyards of homes in the Thiensville area. Water is over Elm St., Green Bay Rd., and N. Cedarburg Rd., and into Thiensville Village Park. Water is near some homes along Island Drive and Shoreland Parkway in the Mequon area. There is general lowland flooding in the Cedarburg and Thiensville areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: River flooding was prevalent across southern WI during the month of September after receiving 12 to 20 inches of rain from August 16th to September 5th. Additional rains throughout the remainder of the month prolonged the flooding. Moderate to major flood stage was reached on the Baraboo and Fox Rivers of central WI, and the Rock River in Jefferson and Rock Counties. Flooding continued into early September on sections of the isthmus in Madison due the high lake levels of Mendota and Monona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood levels were quickly rising on the lower Baraboo River basin and falling in the upper portions of the river basin to begin September. The river crested at moderate flood stage in West Baraboo the morning of September 1st, while the river crested at major flood stage at Baraboo the morning of September 2nd. A new round of 5-9 inches of rain over the upper portion of the Baraboo River basin then resulted in another round of moderate to major flood stage on the river. La Valle crested at moderate flood stage the early morning of September 5th; Reedsburg crested at major flood stage the afternoon of September 5th; Rock Springs crested at major flood stage the morning of September 6th; North Freedom crested the afternoon of September 6th; West Baraboo crested at moderate flood stage the morning of September 7th; Baraboo crested at major flood stage the evening of September 7th. The Baraboo River gradually fell below minor flood stage during the 2nd week of September. Many state and county highways, and bridges, were closed in the communities near and along the Baraboo River. Many homes and businesses sustained minor flood damage in these communities. Thousands of acres of agricultural land were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A home fire caused by a lightning strike resulted in minor to moderate damage to the home.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck the chimney of a home causing the chimney to explode. The debris from the chimney and a fire in the attic caused moderate damage to the home, mainly the roof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The passage of a strong cold front and vigorous upper low pressure area produced gusty gradient winds and numerous rain and snow showers for the morning into the early afternoon hours. Gusty winds of 45 to 55 mph occurred and were most prevalent within the precipitation areas. These strong winds caused high waves on Puckaway Lake and the adjacent Fox River in Green Lake County, where a boat carrying two hunters and a dog capsized. One of the hunters drowned in the river. Another death occurred when a large tree limb fell on a vehicle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered tree limbs down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of heavy rain fell across western Wisconsin on October 8th and 9th with amounts up to two inches. The ground was still saturated from earlier rainfall in late September and early October and the runoff from the rains on the 8th and 9th caused flooding along the Black, Yellow and Grant Rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rains pushed the Yellow River out of its banks in Necedah. The river crested over two feet above the flood stage at 17.54 feet on October 13th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of heavy rain fell across western Wisconsin on October 8th and 9th with amounts up to two inches. The ground was still saturated from earlier rainfall in late September and early October and the runoff from the rains on the 8th and 9th caused flooding along the Black, Yellow and Grant Rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rains pushed the Grant River out of its banks near Burton. The river crested just over a foot above the flood stage at 19.15 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of heavy rain fell across western Wisconsin on October 8th and 9th with amounts up to two inches. The ground was still saturated from earlier rainfall in late September and early October and the runoff from the rains on the 8th and 9th caused flooding along the Black, Yellow and Grant Rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rains pushed the Grant River out of its banks near Burton for the second time in three days. The river crested about a half foot above the flood stage at 18.43 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With the ground already saturated from rain that fell in September, flash flooding occurred on the first day of October after a round of thunderstorms moved across southwest Wisconsin. Rainfall amounts of 1.5 to 3 inches were common across Grant County and the runoff from this rain caused water to go over State Highway 81 east of Cassville and near Ellenboro. Water also went over County Highways E and O along with some township roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With already saturated ground, some flash flooding occurred across Grant County after a round of thunderstorms moved across the area. Water covered several roads including State Highway 81 between Cassville and Beetown and near Ellenboro. County Highway E had water over it between Stitzer and Livingston and County Highway O was closed between Potosi and Platteville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of heavy rain fell across western Wisconsin on October 8th and 9th with amounts up to two inches. The ground was still saturated from earlier rainfall in late September and early October and the runoff from the rains on the 8th and 9th caused flooding along the Black, Yellow and Grant Rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rains pushed the Black River out of its banks in Black River Falls. The river crested almost three feet above the flood stage at 49.88 feet on October 10th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of heavy rain fell across western Wisconsin on October 8th and 9th with amounts up to two inches. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rains pushed the Black River out of its banks near Galesville. The river crested a foot above the flood stage at 13.02 feet on October 13th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced isolated wind damage and hail ahead of a passing cold front. One storm produced a weak tornado near Mattoon (Shawano Co.). In addition, quarter size hail fell near Harshaw (Oneida Co.) and some trees were blown down in southern Langlade County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak, fast-moving tornado caused only minor tree damage (DI 28, DOD 1-3) on the south side of the village of Mattoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure over the South Dakota/Wyoming border during the morning hours moved quickly northeast through the day to just north of Lake Superior by the evening. This low pulled a cold front through the Minnesota Arrowhead region and northwest Wisconsin. An unseasonably warm air mass was located ahead of this strong cold front. Strong shear was also in place which lead to an enhanced tornado risk across northwest Wisconsin, although only hail was reported. Strong winds accompanied the cold front as it moved through the region. Wind gusts to 40-50 mph were reported with the cold front passage, but no damage reports were received.

An autumn storm hit the Northland and brought strong winds, driving rain and some snow. The hardest hit area was the western end of Lake Superior, where winds gusting over 60 mph caused tremendous waves to crash on to shore. The crashing waves and storm surge, aided by an elevated water level of Lake Superior, caused considerable damage to Duluth���s Lakewalk and flooded Duluth���s Canal Park. Traffic was restricted when South Lake Avenue was flooded at the entrance to the aerial lift bridge. There was also major damage to Duluth���s Brighton Beach with damage to the road and major erosion to the shoreline as well as a tremendous amount of debris that was strewn across the park from the powerful waves. The high winds, heavy rain and strong storm surge also caused some county roads to flood along the north shore of Lake Superior and in downtown Grand Marais. Numerous trees and branches were blown down across the Northland, with the hardest hit areas along the north and south shore where winds were the strongest. Many areas lost power. The winds increased during the evening of October 9th with northeast wind gusts reaching over 30 mph. By morning winds gusts were near 50 mph at the Duluth Airport. The winds remained gusty in the 35 to 50 mph range all day and decreased to below 30 mph after 7:00 pm. Some highest wind gusts include 86 mph from the Canadian freighter Algowood off the north shore near Castle Danger, 64 mph and 20 foot waves from the Canadian ship the CSL Assinibione as it was anchored near Duluth, 55 mph in east Duluth, 52 mph at Glensheen Mansion and 46 mph at the Duluth Airport. All of these highest gusts were recorded the morning of October 10th. At 10 am on the 10th, the Areal Lift Bridge in Duluth���s Canal Park recorded a steady wind from 40 to 45 mph and 5 to 9 feet waves. Later that afternoon waves were 10 feet in the ship canal. The storm was caused by a strong low pressure area that developed in the Texas Panhandle on October 9th. By Wednesday, October 10th, the center of the low was over far southern Minnesota while high pressure as situated across much of Canada. Temperatures to the north of the low were in the 40s and south of the low they were in the 70s. This strong gradient in temperature and pressure helped create the strong northeast winds that blew across Lake Superior. The long fetch and the high Lake Superior water level contributed to the high waves and storm surge. Lake Superior levels were almost a foot higher than October normal. By Thursday, October 11th, the low���s center was over eastern Lake Superior which caused the winds to become northwesterly and calmed the Lake Superior waves. Heavy rainfall was also observed with this storm, leading to some minor flooding in northwest Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Max gust recorded at station KSUW, the Superior Airport. The high winds toppled trees and caused power outages. One large pine tree fell on a house in Superior and caused significant roof damage to the home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The lake effect snow of November 9-10 was the first major event along western Lake Superior for the season. Most of the snow fell during the afternoon and evening of the 9th before ending during the early morning hours of the 10th. Snow amounts varied widely from 6 to 24 inches. The heaviest areas were around Mellon in central Ashland County where 10 fell while Gile in northern Iron County recorded 24.1. Other notable totals were 12 in Hurley and 9 at the Bayfield Fish Hatchery.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6 was recorded in a little over 2 hours at Gile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in Vilas County as cold air, circulating around a departing low pressure system, passed over the much warmer waters of Lake Superior. The lake effect snow showers brought an estimated 8 inches of new snow to the northwest part of the county near the Michigan border. A total of 6 inches of new snow was measured at Rest Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of new snow fell at Rest Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An organized and slow moving low pressure area within a very moist airmass resulted in 9 to 15 inches of rain and historic flash flooding from the west side of Madison to Mazomanie, and south to Belleville. State of Emergencies were declared with hundreds of homes flooded and hundreds of citizens evacuated or stranded in the flood waters.  The flash flooding of streets and creeks on the west side of Madison resulted in the drowning death of one man. The already high levels of the Yahara Chain of Lakes reached record levels for the end of August into early September. This resulted in sustained flooding in sections of the isthmus in Madison and lakeshore areas of Lake Monona and Lake Waubesa. In addition to the historic flash flooding, one EF0 tornado occurred in Delavan in Walworth Co. A couple firefighters were indirectly struck by lightning and suffered minor injuries in Cross Plains. Lightning also was the cause of a house fire near Cross Plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The record flooding of Black Earth Creek caused a rail bridge to fail on Wisconsin & Southern Railroad near Blynn Road east of Arena. Sections of railroad tracks were also washed out. The flooding of Blue Mounds Creek near Arena reached Village Edge Road and Helena Road on the north side of the village. Sandbagging and pumping was employed to protect homes. Highway 14 was inundated and closed by flood waters just west of County Road K due to record flooding of Black Earth and Blue Mounds Creeks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms produced widespread wind damage and 18 brief tornadoes across mainly Marquette, Green Lake, Fond du Lac, northern Dodge, and Sheboygan Counties. Flash flooding and river flooding also occurred in several of these areas, especially the Baraboo River in northern Sauk County. The Baraboo River reached major to record flood stage late August into September after 10 to 12 inches of rain over the Baraboo River basin the last two weeks of August.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A swath of widespread tree and crop damage across the southern portion of the county including Endeavor and Montello. Sections of trees and crops completely flattened, and some structural damage from fallen trees or due to the strong downburst winds. Various roads closed due to fallen trees. 2 million dollars in crop damage from straight-line winds, tornadoes, and flooding is estimated throughout the county for late August into early September.

More specifically, a prolonged period of heavy rainfall over northern Jefferson County, far southern Dodge County, and northeast Dane County resulted in 4 to 8 inches of rain and flash flooding. The cities of Watertown and Waterloo experienced the worst of the flooding.

Lowland areas and parks near the Rock River were flooded throughout northern Jefferson County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The widespread flash flooding of creeks and streets in Middleton, Verona, and the west side of Madison resulted in dozens of flooded or stranded vehicles, over 50 water rescues, and hundreds of citizens stranded in place. One drowning death occurred when a man attempted to escape the flood waters by exiting his vehicle. Many city streets were closed including major thoroughfares. 70 residences in the village of Shorewood Hills suffered water damage with 15 families displaced from their homes. Basement flooding was common in Madison and suburbs to the west and south. Water rescues were also executed just west of Verona on County Highway PD due to flash flooding of the Sugar River. Some rural roads south of Madison and Verona were closed due to flooding from the Sugar River or other creeks, including portions of highway 69. Flood waters at Morey Airport in Middleton tipped over a 500 gallon tank of gasoline and a 1,000 gallon tank of jet fuel. The gas tank leaked and was contained by a containment berm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Historic flash flooding of Black Earth Creek and various creeks and low lying areas of western Dane County caused significant flooding impacts. This included hundreds of citizens rescued, evacuated, or stranded, especially along the highway 14 corridor which parallels Black Earth Creek. Homes and businesses in Cross Plains, Black Earth, and Mazomanie were most impacted.  Approximately one half the village of Mazomanie was flooded by Black Earth Creek. 160 homes in Mazomanie were affected while 100 of them experienced water on the 1st floor of the home. 6-12 homes will likely need to be demolished. A bridge was partially washed out on highway 14 near Black Earth, with highway 14 being closed from Middleton to the Iowa County line due to many washouts and high water. Many other roads in western Dane County were closed due to washouts and high water, including southbound 151 near Mount Horeb and large portions of highway 78.  The Black Earth Creek reached record levels of 9.73 feet and 16.6 feet at Black Earth and Mazomanie respectively.

The historic flooding resulted in hundreds of citizens rescued, evacuated, or stranded, especially along the highway 14 corridor which parallels Black Earth Creek and then continues into Middleton and Madison. Homes and businesses in Cross Plains, Black Earth, Mazomanie, Middleton, and the west side of Madison were most impacted. Overall, Dane County had 380 homes with affected damage, 857 homes with minor damage, 302 homes with major damage, and 5 homes destroyed. 27 businesses had minor damage and 17 suffered major damage. |A bridge was partially washed out on highway 14 near Black Earth, with highway 14 being closed from Middleton to the Iowa County line due to many washouts and high water. Much of the flooding was near the Yahara River which connects Lake Mendota to Lake Monona. Additional flooding occurred in lakeshore areas of Lake Mendota, Lake Monona, and Lake Waubesa. Thousands of sandbags were deployed to prevent flood damages. Streets, bike paths, parks, boat launches and piers were closed in the isthmus and other areas in the Yahara Chain of Lakes. This overall flooding continued through the end of August and into early September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving low pressure area within a very moist airmass resulted in rounds of heavy rainfall and flash flooding. More specifically, a prolonged period of heavy rainfall over northern Jefferson County, far southern Dodge County, and northeast Dane County resulted in 4 to 8 inches of rain and flash flooding. The cities of Watertown and Waterloo experienced the worst of the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Approximately 100 911 calls related to flash flooding in Watertown. Numerous streets in Watertown flooded and closed. Some neighborhoods completely flooded with streets, yards, and driveways filled with water. Basement flooding common. A portion of Jefferson Rd. washed out at the bridge where a creek flows into the nearby Rock River. Other road damage or washouts as well. Three vehicle water rescues involving 4 citizens performed by the fire department. A section of track ballast was washed away compromising the railroad ties and tracks. The flooding caused a sanitary sewer overflow/bypass around 10 AM.  Additional street flooding and basement flooding in Waterloo, Ixonia, and northern Jefferson County with some city streets and county roads closed and damaged. Overall, 93 homes sustained affected damage and 66 homes with minor damage. 5 businesses also sustained minor damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of swimmers drown after going swimming at Harrington Beach State Park during a period of dangerous swimming conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ozaukee County Emergency Manager first alerted WFO MKX about a missing swimmer at Harrington Beach State Park along the Lake Michigan shore.  A group of 5 people went swimming in dangerous swimming conditions when strong onshore winds were creating high waves and an increased risk of sub-surface currents.  Of the five swimmers, four people returned to the beach.  Of the four, a woman in her 30s suffered life-threatening injures due to water inhalation, and died.  The missing male swimmer turned up about two weeks later on September 4th at Whitefish Dunes State Park, about 120 miles north along the Lake Michigan shore.  An autopsy confirmed the male died from drowning.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding of streets in sections of Fond du Lac after 2 to 3 inches of rain. Some roads with a depth of 3 feet of water. Intersections of National Ave. and Division St. and National Ave. and Scott St. were closed due to the flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large west to east line of thunderstorms moving east to southeast late on August 27th into the early morning hours of August 28th brought more flash flooding and river flooding to Sauk, Columbia, Marquette, Green Lake, Fond du Lac, Dodge, and Jefferson Counties.  This occurred along a stationary front within a very moist airmass. This rain event was the beginning of significant flooding on the Baraboo and Fox Rivers that continued into September. Flooding also began on the Rock and Crawfish Rivers due to repeated rounds of heavy rain during the last couple weeks of August. This also continued into September.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Crawfish River reached minor flood stage and continued to rise into early September. There was widespread flooding of lowland and agricultural land, and the flooding of roads in Riverbend Campground about 5 miles upstream of Milford.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and branches down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of strong to severe thunderstorms moved across southern WI as a warm front slowly moved north through the region. Flash flooding occurred in Washington, Ozaukee, and northern Milwaukee Counties which was followed by prolonged river flooding. Four to eight inches of rain had fallen over this area. The thunderstorms produced sporadic tree and power line damage across southern WI.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Milwaukee River crested at minor flood stage at 12.2 feet during the early afternoon of August 29th, but continued flooding into the evening of September 3rd. Floodwaters spread into the backyards of homes in the Thiensville area. Water is over Elm St., Green Bay Rd., and N. Cedarburg Rd., and into Thiensville Village Park. Water is near some homes along Island Drive and Shoreland Parkway in the Mequon area. There is general lowland flooding in the Cedarburg and Thiensville areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A death occurred due to a car accident in a construction zone during dense fog.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A car accident during dense fog in a construction zone resulted in a fatality. The accident occurred at the intersection of county highways CW and SC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of swimmers drown after going swimming at Harrington Beach State Park during a period of dangerous swimming conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ozaukee County Emergency Manager first alerted WFO MKX about a missing swimmer at Harrington Beach State Park along the Lake Michigan shore.  A group of 5 people went swimming in dangerous swimming conditions when strong onshore winds were creating high waves and an increased risk of sub-surface currents.  Of the five swimmers, four people returned to the beach.  Of the four, a woman in her 30s suffered life-threatening injures due to water inhalation, and died.  The missing male swimmer turned up about two weeks later, on September 4th at Whitefish Dunes State Park, about 120 miles north along the Lake Michigan shore.  An autopsy confirmed the male died from drowning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An organized and slow moving low pressure area within a very moist airmass resulted in 9 to 15 inches of rain and historic flash flooding from the west side of Madison to Mazomanie, and south to Belleville. State of Emergencies were declared with hundreds of homes flooded and hundreds of citizens evacuated or stranded in the flood waters.  The flash flooding of streets and creeks on the west side of Madison resulted in the drowning death of one man. The already high levels of the Yahara Chain of Lakes reached record levels for the end of August into early September. This resulted in sustained flooding in sections of the isthmus in Madison and lakeshore areas of Lake Monona and Lake Waubesa. In addition to the historic flash flooding, one EF0 tornado occurred in Delavan in Walworth Co. A couple firefighters were indirectly struck by lightning and suffered minor injuries in Cross Plains. Lightning also was the cause of a house fire near Cross Plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage was noted to flags, a large tree limb and metal casing from a US Bank |sign. A small dumpster was also tipped behind the bank. The tornado then tracked to the west northwest through the Wileman Elementary School grounds where numerous large tree limbs were downed. Rubber roofing material on a portion of the school building was lifted and set back in place. The tornado then tracked through residences to the west northwest downing numerous limbs and a rotted/damaged tree. Gutter and roofing damage was noted on one home.

This tornado went east-southeast of the Village of Marquette and was just west of 73.  Widespread straight line winds caused significant tree and crop damage across the county as well.

The flooding of the Baraboo River began to affect a trailer rental business, an auto auction business, and a road wayside park near the intersection of highway 33 and I-90/94.
